coredata.dc:title: Simultaneously self-healing and photoluminescence waterborne polyurethane coatings based on dual dynamic bonds
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Yuan
Wu, Bo
Yuan, Anqian
Fu, Xiaowei
Jiang, Liang
Lei, Jingxin
coredata.dc:description: Waterborne polyurethane coatings capable of excellent transparency, solvent resistance and hardness, have been widely used in furniture, leather and other industries. However, waterborne polyurethane used as coatings may be inevitably damaged during long-term use, which has bad effect on their service life. Therefore, it is of great significance to prepare self-healable waterborne polyurethane coatings with high strength, as well as other new functions. In this work, a class of carboxyl-type waterborne polyurethanes (CWPU) was facilely prepared. The average hydrodynamic diameter size and dispersion index of the prepared CWPU were 56 nm and 0.219, showing uniform size distribution and stable emulsification system. The key point of this work is using polymine (PEI), whose molecular structure containing primary amine and secondary amine can form ionic bonds and hydrogen bonds with CWPU. PEI can be introduced as crosslinking agent to prepare cross-linked CWPU (WPUs). The tensile strength and the elongation at break of WPUs can arrive at 17.12 MPa and 512.25%. After self-healing at 30 °C for 24 h, the tensile strength and elongation at break of the WPUs can reach 3.26 MPa and 450.94%, respectively. Importantly, these synthesized WPUs not only have outstanding mechanical properties, but also exhibit excellent self-healing performance. Additionally, as for coatings, the visible light transmittance of prepared WPUs coatings is all higher than 75%, showing excellent optical properties. Especially, incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQs) can endow these prepared WPUs with obvious photoluminescence performance. The aforementioned functions of self-healing and photoluminescence performance have expanded the service life and application of WPUs coatings. This kind of coatings is expected to be used in plexiglass protective coating, light emitting diode, road signs, optical anti-counterfeiting and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waterborne polyurethane
Carbon quantum dots
Coatings
Self-healing
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PEI-type waterborne polyurethane solution
Preparation of waterborne polyurethane/carbon quantum dot (WPU/CQs) mixture and coating
Mechanical properties, transparency, and thermodynamic properties of WPUs
Photoluminescence performance of WPU-3/CQs dispersion solution and luminescent coating

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financially supported by State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering (Grant No. sklpme2020-2-5 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images at different magnification, (b) The emulsion particle size distribution and photograph of CWPU.
(a) FTIR spectra of PTMG, IPDI, Prepolymer and WPUs. (b) FTIR spectra of WPU-0, WPU-1, WPU-2, WPU-3 and WPU-4.
Photographic images of WPUs before and after being immersed into tetrahydrofuran at 60 °C for 24 h.
(a) Tensile stress-strain curves of WPUs, (b) Transmittance of WPUs and photograph of WPU-3 coating, (c) Tan-Delta-temperature curves, (d) DSC thermogram of WPUs.
Stress-relaxation curves of WPUs at (a) 30 °C and (b) 60 °C, Stress-relaxation curves of (c) WPU-0 and (d) WPU-3 at various temperature.

(a) Photographs of WPU-3 film lifting a 1 kg weight before and after self-healing. (b) Tensile stress-strain curves of self-healed WPUs. (c) Self-healing efficiency of WPUs at room temperature. (d) SEM images of WPU-3 before and after self-healing, scale bar is 100 μm. (e) Schematic illustration of the self-healing mechanism of WPUs.
(a) Transmission spectra of WPU-3 film at different state. (b) The photographic images of pristine, scratched and self-healed of WPU-3 coating.
(a) Photographs of WPU-3, WPU-3/CQs emulsion and coatings before and after UV irradiation. PL emission spectra of (b) WPU-3/CQs emulsion and (c) coating at different excitation wavelengths.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Represents the mass ratio of m (CWPU) × 30 wt% and m (PEI) × 25 wt%.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Contact angle of WPUs films after vacuum drying at room temperature for 2 h and then subsequent storage for 0 h, 6 h, 12 h and 24 h in the atmosphere (ca. 25 °C; humidity: ca. 60%).

head.ce:title: Simultaneously self-healing and photoluminescence waterborne polyurethane coatings based on dual dynamic bonds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, Polymer Research Institute of Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering
Polymer Research Institute of Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, Polymer Research Institute of Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waterborne polyurethane coatings capable of excellent transparency, solvent resistance and hardness, have been widely used in furniture, leather and other industries. However, waterborne polyurethane used as coatings may be inevitably damaged during long-term use, which has bad effect on their service life. Therefore, it is of great significance to prepare self-healable waterborne polyurethane coatings with high strength, as well as other new functions. In this work, a class of carboxyl-type waterborne polyurethanes (CWPU) was facilely prepared. The average hydrodynamic diameter size and dispersion index of the prepared CWPU were 56 nm and 0.219, showing uniform size distribution and stable emulsification system. The key point of this work is using polymine (PEI), whose molecular structure containing primary amine and secondary amine can form ionic bonds and hydrogen bonds with CWPU. PEI can be introduced as crosslinking agent to prepare cross-linked CWPU (WPUs). The tensile strength and the elongation at break of WPUs can arrive at 17.12 MPa and 512.25%. After self-healing at 30 °C for 24 h, the tensile strength and elongation at break of the WPUs can reach 3.26 MPa and 450.94%, respectively. Importantly, these synthesized WPUs not only have outstanding mechanical properties, but also exhibit excellent self-healing performance. Additionally, as for coatings, the visible light transmittance of prepared WPUs coatings is all higher than 75%, showing excellent optical properties. Especially, incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQs) can endow these prepared WPUs with obvious photoluminescence performance. The aforementioned functions of self-healing and photoluminescence performance have expanded the service life and application of WPUs coatings. This kind of coatings is expected to be used in plexiglass protective coating, light emitting diode, road signs, optical anti-counterfeiting and other fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Waterborne polyurethane coatings use water as dispersion medium, will not release a large amount of organic solvent in the process of preparation and use. These waterborne polyurethanes not only have excellent mechanical properties compared with traditional solvent-based polyurethanes, but also are environmentally friendly materials without any solvent releasing [1–3]. However, waterborne polyurethanes used as coating, may be inevitably damaged during long-term use. These damages will have bad effect on its mechanical strength, corrosion resistances and service life [4,5]. Usually, waterborne polyurethane coatings have high glass transition temperature, leading to the high self-healing temperature. Especially, polymer materials that are used as coatings applied in furniture industry, cannot withstand high temperature and difficult self-healing ability. Therefore, it is of great value to develop waterborne polyurethane coatings with self-healable property at room temperature.

Recently, introducing dynamic interaction including hydrogen bonds [6–8], dynamic covalent bonds [9,10] and dynamic ionic bonds [11–13] into the molecular chain can be used to realize self-healing performance. Among them, dynamic ionic bonds are the electrostatic interaction between charged ions (that is, cation and anion), which have many advantages compared with other dynamic bonds. For instance, ionic bonds can interact in different directions, meanwhile a single ion can interact with multiple ions with opposite charges, showing strong interactions [14–16]. Recently, most researches introduce the dynamic ionic bonds to construct self-healing materials based on metal ions. Zuo et al. introduced Zn2+ into polydimethylsiloxane to produce self-healing cross-linked elastomers based on the dynamic interaction between Zn2+ and carboxylate ions, which has potential application in surgical correction [17]. Li et al. prepared double-network hydrogels with high strength, toughness, self-recovery, healing and 3D printing processability based on the reversible nature of ionic cross-linking between Fe3+ and carboxylate ions [18]. Kalista et al. used Na+ to cross-link poly (ethylene-co-methacrylic acid) copolymer for preparing a specific class of poly (ethylene-co-methacrylic acid) copolymers and ionomers capable of a unique ability to instantly self-heal following ballistic projectile puncture [19]. Although great progress has been in the development of self-healing performance by introducing dynamic ionic bonds, the reaction speed between metal cation and anion group is very fast, leading to the rapid increase of viscosity and easy agglomeration. Therefore, incorporation of dynamic ionic bonds into water-based polymers, which is deemed to use as coatings, has an adverse effect upon practical application.

Polyethylenimine (PEI) is also called polyazocyclopropane, containing a large number of functional groups of primary amine, secondary amine and tertiary amine in the molecular structure. These functional groups can react with anionic groups including carboxylic acid and sulfonic acid to form ionic bonds [20,21]. Li and coworkers developed a self-healing coating by wrapping the highly branched PEI to obtain the nanocontainers [22]. It was reported that a self-healing branched polyethylenimine (bPEI)/poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) films is prepared by layer-by-layer films by using colloidal lithography and photolithography [23]. PEI capable of active groups is good candidate for constructing dynamic ionic bonds for waterborne coatings, which can avoid using metal cation. To our best knowledge, incorporating PEI into polymer matrix to devise mechanical robust and intrinsic self-healing network has not reported.

In this paper, we prepared a class of carboxyl-type waterborne polyurethanes (CWPU) by using commercial raw materials through the copolymerization between polytetrahydrofuran ether diol, isophorone diisocyanate, 2,2-bis(hydroxymethyl)propionic acid. Additionally, different amount of PEI is added into CWPU to prepare cross-linked CWPU (WPUs). PEI capable of a large number of amine groups plays the role of both cross-linking agent and plasticizer. The hydrophilicity and flexibility of WPUs can be adjusted by the amount of PEI contents, meeting the needs of different coatings. For instance, flexible ones are good for artificial leather, while hard ones for furniture. Significantly, these WPUs have outstanding self-healing performance at room temperature because the molecular structure of PEI containing primary amine and secondary amine can form ionic bonds and hydrogen bonds with CWPU. Especially, these designed waterborne polyurethane/carbon quantum dots (WPU/CQs) have excellent photoluminescence performance after incorporating carbon quantum dots into the matrix of pristine WPUs. The chemical structure, mechanical properties, thermal properties, self-healing properties and fluorescence properties of the devised WPUs were detailedly studied. The designed strategy provides a facile approach to prepare solvent-free WPUs with simultaneously self-healing and photoluminescence.

Polytetrahydrofuran ether diols (PTMG, M n = 1000 g/mol, analytically pure) were obtained from Mitsubishi Chemical Corporation (Japan). Isoferone diisocyanate (IPDI, analytically pure), dimethylolpropionic acid (DMPA, analytically pure) and polyetheramine-230 (D-230, analytically pure) were offered by Alighting Reagent (China) Co., LTD (China). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, analytically pure) was provided by Jining Baichuan Chemical Co., LTD (China). Triethylamine (TEA, analyte pure), dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL, analyte pure) and citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, analyte pure) were purchased by Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent (China). Aqueous polymine (PEI-3000, analyte pure, solid content is 25 wt%) was provided by Guangdong Wengjiang Chemical Reagent Co., LTD (China). PTMG was dried under vacuum at 110 °C for 1 h before use.

Into a 250 ml three-mouth flask, 20 g of PTMG-1000 was added and melted at 60 °C. After that, 13.2 g of IPDI, 10 g of DMF solution (DMF accounted for 5% of the total mass of the reactants) and 12.12 mg of DBTDL were added. The reaction proceeded at 60 °C for 0.5 h and then at 80 °C for further 4 h. The reaction mixture was cooled down to 60 °C and then 1.38 g of TEA was added with subsequent stirring for 0.5 h. The reaction mixtures were further cooled down to 40 °C and 76.19 g of deionized water was rapidly added to fully disperse the prepolymers. After that, 2.09 g D-230 in 5 g of water was gradually dripped into it for 20 min. After continuous stirring for 3 h, CWPU emulsion was obtained. The solid content of CWPU was 30 wt%, and the content of -COOH of solid was 7 × 10−4 mol/g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PEI-type waterborne polyurethane solution
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g ammonia was firstly added into quantified CWPU emulsion to avoid forming gel. Then, a stoichiometric amount of PEI was added, followed by fully stirring. Finally, WPUs emulsion was prepared and poured into a poly tetra fluoroethylene mould through solution casting to form films. The samples were named WPU-0, WPU-1, WPU-2, WPU-3 and WPU-4 according to the solid content ratio of CWPU and PEI. Among them, WPU-0 was acted as the control group. The synthetic route was shown in Fig. 1 , and the specific formula of each sample was shown in Table 1 . Meanwhile, WPUs was coated on a quartz glass separately with wire rod to prepare WPUs coatings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of waterborne polyurethane/carbon quantum dot (WPU/CQs) mixture and coating
ce:section.ce:para: According to the methods reported in literature [24], CQs was prepared via citric acid monohydrate, which can emit blue fluorescence. A certain amount of CQs powder was added into WPU-3 emulsion (CQs powder accounted for 0.05% of the solid quality), and then fully dispersed under ultrasonic condition. The mixture was coated on quartz glass by wire rod to prepare luminescent composite coatings, namely WPU-3/CQs.

The particle size of CWPU emulsion was measured by dynamic light scattering particle analyzer (Masterssize 2000, Malvern, England) at room temperature. The test range was 0.02–2000 μm, and the repeatability was less than ±1%RSD (relative standard deviation). The morphology and particle size of CWPU emulsion was observed by Tecnai G2-F20 transmission electron microscope, and the emulsion was diluted with distilled water to a solid content of 5%. Hydrogen nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) (400 MHz) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance (Switzerland) with tetramethylsilane TMS as the internal standard at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet-560 (USA) at room temperature with spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1 and resolution of 4 cm−1. The thermodynamic performance of WPUs film was characterized by a dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA, Q800) from TA Instruments (USA) in strain mode with a frequency of 1 Hz and a amplitude of 20 μm from −70 °C to 180 °C at 5 °C/min. The stress relaxation behavior of the sample was also tested by DMA in a stress relaxation mode at the setting strain of 10% at the set temperature. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analyzer (DSC 204) was used for testing the glass-transition temperature (T g) in nitrogen atmosphere. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy were performed on a UV-3600 UV–visible spectrophotometer (USA) at room temperature at a rate of 60 nm/min in the wavelength range from 800 nm to 200 nm. Mechanical properties were tested on an Instron-4302 electronic universal testing machine loaded with a 1 kN load cell at a rate of 50 mm/min. At least three dumbbell-shaped specimens were prepared for each testing. TGA was carried out on the SDT-Q600 thermo-analyzer instrument from 30 °C to 600 °C under nitrogen atmosphere with heating rate of 10 °C/min. Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometers were used to test the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQs.

The self-healing performance was conducted as followed: the dumbbell samples of WPU-0 to WPU-4 were cut into two segments with a razor blade, and placed without applying force. After that, the two segments were firstly put at 30 °C for 24 h, then dried in a vacuum oven at 30 °C for 6 h for the mechanical properties test.

The solvent resistance test of the prepared cross-linked WPUs was conducted that a certain amount of each film (0.45–0.55 g) was weighed and soaked in tetrahydrofuran at 60 °C and then changed the solvent every 6 h. The whole process was recorded by a digital camera.

Fig. 2 a shows the TEM images of CWPU emulsion. The particle morphology apparently presents uniform sphere, especially the surface of the particle shows smooth. The average diameter of the particles in TEM is approximately 50 nm. Fig. 2b shows the particle size distribution of CWPU emulsion. The results show that the particle size of CWPU emulsion presents a single peak distribution. The dispersion index (PDI) is 0.219 and the average hydrodynamic diameter size in dynamic light scattering (DLS) is 56 nm, which is in agreement with the tested results by TEM. The above results indicate that the devised CWPU can be uniformly dispersed in water.

FTIR spectra of PTMG, IPDI, prepolymer and WPU-0 are shown in Fig. 3 a. As for the spectrogram of PTMG, the characteristic absorption band of -OH in PTMG is located at 3459 cm−1. The characteristic absorption bands at 2939 cm−1 and 2861 cm−1 are attributed to antisymmetric stretching vibration and symmetrical stretching vibration of methylene CH in PTMG. And the stretching vibration absorption band of C-O-C in ether bond exist at 1112 cm−1. In the spectrogram of IPDI, the absorption band at 2259 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption band of -NCO in the IPDI molecular chain. For the spectrogram of prepolymer, the absorption bands at 3330 cm−1, 2939 cm−1, 2861 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 are respectively classified as characteristic absorption band of -OH, anti-symmetric stretching vibration absorption band of -CH2, symmetric stretching vibration band of -CH2 and stretching vibration band of C-O-C, which is corresponding to the spectrogram of PTMG. Meanwhile, the absorption band at 2259 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption band of -NCO, which is corresponding to the spectrogram of IPDI. These results indicate that the prepolymer has been successfully synthesized by PTMG and IPDI. As shown in the spectrogram of WPU-0, the absorption bands at 1709 cm−1 and 1651 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO in the carbamate bond and the urea bond, respectively. The absorption bands at 3327 cm−1, 2939 cm−1, 2774 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 in the prepolymer are all retained, but the characteristic absorption band of -NCO in the IPDI molecular chain disappeared, indicating the successful synthesis of WPUs. Additionally, the 1H NMR results further confirm that WPU-0 has been successfully synthesized by PTMG, IPDI and DMPA, which is consistent with the FTIR results (Supporting Information Fig. S1). As can be seen from Fig. 3b, the absorption band at 1700 cm−1 gradually decreases from WPU-0 to WPU-4, while the absorption band located at about 1600 cm−1 gradually increases. The reason for this is that the absorption band of carboxylated groups increases with the amount of PEI increasing, resulting in formation of more ionic bonds between CWPU and PEI [25].

The results of solvent resistance experiments are investigated according to the previously reported method [11,26,27]. As depicted in Fig. 4 , the swelling test shows that the sample of WPU-0 is completely dissolved, forming a homogeneous solution after soaking in tetrahydrofuran at 60 °C for 24 h. While the samples of WPU-1, WPU-2, WPU-3 and WPU-4 are unsolvable and only slightly swell in the same conditions, indicating that the prepared CWPU cross-linked by PEI have excellent solvent resistance and formed a relatively stable cross-linked structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties, transparency, and thermodynamic properties of WPUs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, when the mass ratio of CWPU and PEI is 9.5:0.5, the tensile strength and the elongation at break of WPU-1 are 17.12 MPa and 512.25%, respectively. The tensile strength is far higher than that of WPU-0 without PEI at the value of 5.23 MPa, while the elongation at break is far lower than that of WPU-0 at the value of 918.77%. This is because a small amount of PEI mainly acts as a crosslinker, limiting the slippage of the polymer chain [28–30]. Thus, WPU-1 exhibits higher mechanical strength. However, due to the limited movement ability of polymer molecular chain, it is unable to produce corresponding deformation under external force. Thus, fracture occurs under the action of smaller external force, resulting in the reduction of elongation at break. As from WPU-2 to WPU-4, with the increase of PEI contents, the tensile strength of the samples gradually decreases while the elongation at break gradually increases. The results are that PEI is a small and weak molecule without strength, which can absorb moisture from air, acting as plasticizer. When the additive amount exceeds a certain value, the plasticizing effect of PEI gets greater than the crosslinking effect, which makes the polymer molecular chain easier to slip and the motion ability enhances. Therefore, the tensile strength decreases yet the elongation at break increases. The transmittance of the WPUs coatings is shown in Fig. 5b. The transmittance of all the samples is above 75% in the range of visible light. Additionally, the insert photograph of WPU-3 in Fig. 5b also shows obvious transmittance, illustrating the excellent transparency of WPUs. Among them, WPU-0 has the highest transmittance, as WPU-0 is linear polymer without PEI because of the smallest ion microregion and least obvious phase separation [31].

Fig. 5c and d are the tan delta-temperature curves and the DSC test results of WPUs. With the increase of PEI, T g of WPUs firstly increases from WPU-0 to WPU-1 and then decreases from WPU-1 to WPU-4 with the maximum value of 19.25 °C. This is because PEI plays the role of both crosslinker and plasticizer. From WPU-0 to WPU-1, the amount of PEI is very low in whole mass of WPUs. Thus, the cross-linking effect of PEI restricts the motion ability of polymer chain, leading to T g of WPU-1 increasing compared with that of WPU-0. However, with the amount of PEI content continue increasing (from WPU-2 to WPU-4), the plasticizing effect becomes greater than the crosslinking effect. The van der Waals force between the molecular chains is weakened, and the mobility of the molecular chains enhances. Therefore, the chains can move at a lower temperature, leading to the decrease of T g. The above results also indicate that the scratch hardness of prepared WPUs can be adjustable.

The stress relaxation curves of WPUs at 30 °C and 60 °C are shown in Fig. 6 a and b. Fig. 6c and d shows the stress relaxation curves of WPU-0 and WPU-3 at different temperatures. The time required for stress relaxation to 36.8% (1/e) of the initial value is generally defined as the stress relaxation time [32,33]. In Fig. 6a and b, when the tested temperature at 30 °C and 60 °C, that the stress relaxation time of WPUs firstly increases from 9.65 min and 2.15 min for WPU-0 to 10.50 min and 7.72 min for WPU-1, and then decreases to the minimum value of 1.83 min and 0.77 min for WPU-4. Obviously, the relaxation time of WPU-1 is the maximum value of 10.50 min and 7.72 min. The results are that PEI is mainly acted as cross-linking agent at relatively low contents, limiting the movement of molecular chains and leading to an increase in the time required for the internal stress to drop to 1/e under the same condition. However, after further increasing the amount of PEI contents, PEI mainly plays a dominant role in plasticization, which promotes the conformational changes of molecular chains, meanwhile, the number of dynamic ionic bonds in system increases, making the dynamic exchange reaction more likely to occur and leading to the decrease in the stress relaxation time. In Fig. 6c and d, the stress relaxation time of WPU-0 and WPU-3 gradually decreases from 9.65 min and 2.09 min at 30 °C to 0.41 min and 0.37 min at 90 °C, respectively, which is attributed to the tested temperature that promotes the reversible exchange reaction between dynamic bonds of hydrogen bond and ionic bond Thus, the topological rearrangement time of the cross-linked network is shorter at higher temperature, leading to shorter stress relaxation time [34].

It can be seen from Fig. 7 a that the self-healed spline can lift 1 kg weight, demonstrating that WPUs have good self-healing performance at room temperature from a macro perspective. Fig. 7b is the tensile stress-strain curve of WPUs after healing, and Fig. 7c is the bar chart of the self-healing efficiency for WPUs. As for the control group of WPU-0, the self-healing efficiency of tensile strength and elongation at break are 26.77% and 23.36%, which are both higher than that of WPU-1 at 12.68% for tensile strength and 21.92% for elongation at break. This is due to the low addition of PEI in WPU-1, leading to low concentration of dynamic ionic bond. Additionally, the cross-linking effect of PEI reduces the motion ability of the molecular chain, meanwhile the self-healed spline cannot produce corresponding deformation under external force, resulting in low self-healing efficiency. However, from WPU-2 to WPU-4, the self-healing efficiency of WPUs gradually increases and all are higher than that of WPU-0, displaying great self-healing performance of WPUs. In addition, it can be seen that WPU-3 has the best mechanical properties after healing with the tensile strength up to 3.26 MPa and the elongation at break up to 450.94%. Fig. 7d is the SEM images of WPU-3 before and after self-healing. It can be seen that the crack almost completely disappeared after self-healing at 30 °C for 24 h, further showing that WPU-3 has excellent self-healing performance. Based on the above analysis, the self-healing mechanism diagram of WPUs can be deduced, as shown in Fig. 7e. On the one hand, with the increase of the amount of PEI, the dynamic ionic bond concentration in the system increases, showing that the dynamic exchange reaction can take place more fully. On the other hand, PEI contains a large number of hydrophilic group -NH2, which can absorb water vapor in the air. The introduction of water molecules acts as plasticizer, filling the gaps in the WPUs network and producing a swelling effect. Therefore, water improves the conformational changes of molecular chain, making it easier for molecules on the fracture surface to get close to each other and spread. Subsequently, the samples are dried in a vacuum oven. With water being removed, WPUs chains were driven to penetrate each other, so the dynamic ionic bond in the system and hydrogen bond acted sites without forming bound water can form bonds again towards realizing outstanding self-healing performance.

To further verify the hydrophilicity of WPUs in air, the samples were placed into a vacuum oven at room temperature for 12 h to remove water, followed by testing the contact angle immediately. Additionally, the contact angle of WPUs was tested after these samples were put in air for 6 h, 12 h and 24 h. As shown in Table 2 , the contact angle of each sample gradually increases with the increase of time, confirming the excellent hydrophilicity of WPUs samples. From WPU-0 to WPU-4, the contact angle of these samples gradually decreases with more PEI adding. Importantly, the longer the time placed in air, the greater the reduction of the contact angle. The contact angle of WPU-4 is only 44.9° after being placed in air for 24 h. This is because PEI contains a large number of hydrophilic groups that can absorb massive water in the air.

Although WPUs coatings has high transparency, it may be affected by external corruption during use. Therefore, research on transmittance of prepared WPUs before and after self-healing is of great significance, the WPU-3 was selected to create scratches on the surface with a copper brush. After that, the scratched sample was self-healed in an oven at 30 °C for 24 h. The transmittance of original, scratched, and self-healed WPU-3 coating was tested respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, the surface of original WPU-3 coating is smooth and the transmittance of original WPU-3 coating can reach at 76.76%. After scratching, the transmittance of scratched WPU-3 coating obviously decreased to 17.09%, meanwhile apparently scratches appeared on the surface of scratched WPU-3 coating. But, after self-healing, the transmittance of healed WPU-3 coating can recover to 65.41%. Especially, the scratches on the surface of healed WPU-3 coating basically disappeared, meanwhile the healed WPU-3 coating became transparent again, which is consistent with original WPU-3 coating. The results indicate that WPU-3 coating can keep exceptional transmittance due to its excellent self-healing performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence performance of WPU-3/CQs dispersion solution and luminescent coating
ce:section.ce:para: In order to render WPU-3 coating more functions, WPU-3/CQs coating was specially prepared by adding CQs into pristine WPUs matrix. CQs contains a large number of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface, which can interact with the polar groups on the polymer chain and can be dissolved in WPU-3 emulsion steadily to obtain the WPU-3/CQs mixture. Then, WPU-3/CQs coating can be obtained by simply coating it on quartz glass through wire rod and drying. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the pure WPU-3 material did not emit luminescence under 365 nm UV light, while the WPU-3/CQs emulsion and coating could emit bright blue fluorescence, indicating that the prepared WPU-3/CQs emulsion and coating have new function of good photoluminescence.

Fig. 9b and c shows the fluorescence emission spectra of WPU-3/CQs emulsion and coating at different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence emission intensity of WPU-3/CQs emulsion and coating firstly increases from 329,411 a.u. and 37,974 a.u. to 1,044,776 a.u. and 142,955 a.u., and then decreases to 214,223 a.u. and 31,091 a.u. with the excitation wavelength increasing from 320 nm to 360 nm and then decreasing to 400 nm, the maximum excitation wavelength is 360 nm, which is consistent with previous studies [35]. The maximum emission wavelength of CQs is redshifted with the increase of citation wavelength. This means that the fluorescence emission of CQs has a certain wavelength dependence of excitation. Additionally, the mechanical properties of WPU-3/CQs were tested to investigate the effect of CQs (Supporting Information Fig. S2). The results show that the stress-strain curves of WPU-3 is in accord with that of WPU-3/CQs, indicating that the addition of CQs has almost no effect on the mechanical properties of WPU-3. According to the above analysis, self-healing polymer coating with photoluminescence function has been successfully prepared, which is expected to be used in LED lighting, road signs, optical anti-counterfeiting and other fields.

In summary, carboxyl waterborne polyurethane (CWPU) with excellent dispersibility in water have been successfully prepared by using commercial raw materials containing PTMG, IPDI, DMPA, TEA and D230. Additionally, a series of PEI-type waterborne polyurethanes (WPUs) were prepared by incorporating PEI into aforementioned pristine CWPU. WPUs has excellent mechanical properties, thermal stability and transparency. With the increase of PEI, T g of WPUs firstly increases and then decreases. PEI plays the role of both crosslinker and plasticizer, endowing the material with good self-healing performance at room temperature. After healing, the material has good mechanical properties with tensile strength of 3.26 MPa and elongation at break of 450.94%, which meet the requirements of conventional coating. CQs that was prepared by citric acid monohydrate and ethylenediamine, was added into WPUs to prepare WPU-3/CQs coating, exhibiting excellent self-healing properties and photoluminescence functions. Especially, the WPU-3/CQs coating can emit bright blue fluorescence under UV light, and the fluorescence emission intensity reaches 143,564.53 a.u under the maximum excitation wavelength. This kind of materials take the advantages of simple preparation technology, no release of organic solvent during use, non-toxicity and harmlessness, and environmental-friendly nature, which is expected to be used in plexiglass protective coating, LED lighting, road signs, optical anti-counterfeiting and other fields.

Jingxin Lei and Liang Jiang designed the experiments. Yuan Lei designed the experiments, conducted the experiments, analyzed the experimental data, wrote the manuscript and revised the manuscript. Bo Wu, Anqian Yuan, and Xiaowei Fu analyzed the experimental data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financially supported by State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering (Grant No. sklpme2020-2-5).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2021.106433.
